skill.name: scanning-tools
skill.description: Guides authorized security teams through common vulnerability scanning, web testing, wireless assessment, cloud auditing, malware scanning, and compliance tools.
skill.summary: Reference guide for authorized security scanning workflows and reporting.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static findings for external commands, network activity, privileged commands, Metasploit references, and scanner tooling are largely true positives in context. The file is a dual-use security scanning guide rather than executable malware, and no prompt injection or credential exfiltration was found, but it includes offensive workflows and should not be published without strict controls.

high_findings.title: Offensive Network Scanning Guidance
high_findings.description: The guide provides copy-ready Nmap and Masscan workflows for host discovery, stealth scans, full port scans, vulnerability scripts, banner grabbing, and an internet-wide Masscan example. This is legitimate for authorized testing but can enable unauthorized reconnaissance and target enumeration.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands and descriptions directly show active scanning, stealth scanning, vulnerability scripts, and high-rate Masscan usage. The risk is clear even though authorized use is possible.

high_findings.title: Active Vulnerability and Web Application Scanning
high_findings.description: The skill includes Nessus, OpenVAS, ZAP, Nikto, and Docker-based ZAP workflows for active vulnerability discovery against target networks and web applications. These scanners can generate intrusive traffic and should be restricted to authorized scopes.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections contain scanner launch commands and target parameters. The purpose is vulnerability assessment, which is dual-use and potentially disruptive.

high_findings.title: Wireless Deauthentication and Password Cracking Workflow
high_findings.description: The Aircrack-ng section includes monitor mode, packet capture, deauthentication attack, WPA handshake cracking, and WEP cracking steps. This can disrupt wireless networks and support unauthorized credential attacks.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow explicitly includes deauthentication and wireless cracking commands. These are offensive techniques with a direct misuse path.

high_findings.title: Metasploit-Based Vulnerability Validation
high_findings.description: The skill instructs users to start Metasploit, import scan data, use an SMB scanner module, run it against a target range, and analyze vulnerabilities for exploit suggestions. This confirms the static Metasploit blocker as a meaningful high-risk dual-use finding.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited section names Metasploit and gives operational steps for scanner modules and exploit suggestion. It is not hidden malware, but it is clearly offensive security guidance.

medium_findings.title: Privileged and System-Modifying Commands
medium_findings.description: Several examples require sudo or modify local system state, including installing scanners, starting services, updating malware definitions, running host audits, writing reports, and moving or removing infected files. These commands can affect host integrity if run without review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The privileged commands are directly visible and many are normal administration actions. Risk depends on execution context and authorization.

medium_findings.title: Credentialed Cloud Security Assessment Guidance
medium_findings.description: The Prowler and ScoutSuite sections guide users through cloud account assessment and role assumption workflows. This is useful for defenders, but it requires access to sensitive cloud credentials or roles and can expose account configuration data.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The commands are standard defensive audit tools, but the assumed role and cloud scan context imply sensitive permissions. No exfiltration endpoint was found.

medium_findings.title: API Key Placeholder in Web Scanner Configuration
medium_findings.description: The ZAP API mode example includes an api.key placeholder. This is not a hardcoded secret, but users could expose a real key if they copy the pattern into shared logs or prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The scanner flagged certificate or key material, but the cited value is a placeholder. The residual concern is user handling of real API keys.

low_findings.title: Example IP Addresses and Local URLs
low_findings.description: Many hardcoded IP addresses and URLs are documentation examples using private network ranges, localhost services, or placeholder targets. They do not show unauthorized outbound communication or data exfiltration by the skill itself.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited values are examples in Markdown and include localhost or placeholder domains. They are still relevant because users may replace them with real targets.

low_findings.title: False Positive Keyword Blockers
low_findings.description: The static Cobalt Strike, weak cryptography, and email capability hits appear to be keyword matches without supporting malicious context. No prompt injection, covert command execution, credential theft, or exfiltration endpoint was found in the reviewed file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line context supports benign documentation or table content for these specific keyword hits. Confidence is not perfect because the static analyzer did not expose exact matched tokens.

dangerous_patterns.title: Internet-Scale High-Rate Scan Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The Masscan section includes a scan against 0.0.0.0/0 with a high packet rate. This pattern can affect third-party systems if copied outside an authorized scope.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The target range and rate are explicit. This is a concrete high-risk scanning pattern.

dangerous_patterns.title: Stealth and IDS-Evasion Adjacent Scan Options
dangerous_patterns.description: The guide includes SYN scan, paranoid timing, aggressive timing, vulnerability scripts, and stealth scan references. These options can support evasive or unauthorized reconnaissance.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line context documents scan types commonly used in both legitimate testing and stealthy reconnaissance. The risk is contextual but real.

dangerous_patterns.title: Wireless Disruption and Cracking Commands
dangerous_patterns.description: The Aircrack-ng workflow includes deauthentication and cracking examples. These can disrupt clients and support unauthorized wireless access attempts.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines directly describe deauthentication and cracking actions. There is little ambiguity in the capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: Exploit Framework Operational Steps
dangerous_patterns.description: The Metasploit section provides operational framework steps, target range configuration, and exploit suggestion commands. This is high-risk dual-use content even without a payload example.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: Metasploit usage and target configuration are explicit. The section stops short of payload execution but still enables exploitation validation.

dangerous_patterns.title: Host File Modification During Malware Scans
dangerous_patterns.description: The ClamAV examples include moving infected files and removing files during recursive scans. These actions can delete or relocate data if copied without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file modification flags are visible and potentially destructive. The commands are legitimate antivirus operations when used carefully.

content.user_title: Assess Authorized Systems with Scanning Tools
content.value_statement: Security teams need a clear way to choose the right scanner for each authorized assessment. This skill organizes common tools, workflows, reporting steps, and safety limits for structured scanning.
actual_capabilities: Explains when to use Nmap, Masscan, Nessus, OpenVAS, ZAP, Nikto, Aircrack-ng, Kismet, Metasploit, Prowler, ScoutSuite, Lynis, and OpenSCAP.
Provides example workflows for host discovery, port scanning, vulnerability scanning, web application testing, wireless assessment, cloud auditing, and compliance checks.
Shows common output formats and report generation approaches for scan results.
Gives a phased methodology for planning, discovery, vulnerability assessment, analysis, and reporting.
Includes troubleshooting guidance for missed hosts, slow scans, and incomplete web scanner coverage.
limitations: It does not verify that a target is authorized before scanning begins.
It does not install or run scanner tools by itself.
Some examples are intrusive and can disrupt systems if copied without controls.
It does not replace expert validation of scan findings or legal review.

use_cases.title: Plan an Internal Vulnerability Assessment
use_cases.description: Select appropriate discovery, vulnerability scanning, and reporting tools for an approved internal network assessment.

use_cases.title: Prepare a Web Application Test
use_cases.description: Compare ZAP, Burp Suite, and Nikto workflows for an authorized web application review.

use_cases.title: Document Compliance Evidence
use_cases.description: Map Lynis, OpenSCAP, Prowler, and ScoutSuite outputs to audit documentation and remediation tracking.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Scanner
prompt_templates.prompt: I have written authorization to assess this environment. Help me choose safe scanning tools for the scope, goals, and reporting needs I describe.

prompt_templates.title: Build a Scan Plan
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a phased scanning plan for an authorized internal assessment. Include discovery, vulnerability checks, validation, reporting, and safety controls.

prompt_templates.title: Review Scanner Results
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me interpret these authorized scan results. Group findings by risk, identify likely false positives, and suggest validation steps.

prompt_templates.title: Design a Compliance Workflow
prompt_templates.prompt: Design an evidence collection workflow for an authorized compliance audit using host, cloud, and vulnerability scanning outputs.

output_examples.input: Plan an authorized internal scan for a small office network.
output: Recommended approach: start with low-impact discovery, confirm live hosts, then run targeted service checks.
Risk controls: use written scope, avoid internet-wide ranges, limit scan rate, and schedule intrusive checks during an approved window.
Deliverables: host inventory, service list, validated findings, risk summary, and remediation recommendations.

output_examples.input: Summarize web application scanner findings for a report.
output: Executive summary: the test identified outdated server software, missing security headers, and possible input validation issues.
Validation plan: reproduce each finding manually, remove duplicates, and confirm exploitability before assigning severity.
Recommended next steps: patch outdated components, add missing headers, and retest affected workflows.

output_examples.input: Prepare compliance evidence from host and cloud scans.
output: Evidence package: scanner scope, tool versions, scan dates, affected assets, control mappings, and remediation status.
Review notes: separate policy gaps from technical vulnerabilities and track exceptions with business owners.
Audit caveat: scanner output supports evidence collection but does not prove full compliance alone.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, scope, timing, and rate limits before any scan.
Start with low-impact discovery and increase scan depth only when approved.
Validate scanner findings manually before reporting severity or remediation deadlines.
anti_patterns: Do not scan public or third-party networks without explicit written permission.
Do not paste real credentials, API keys, or cloud role details into prompts.
Do not run destructive, deauthentication, or exploit validation steps in production without a controlled test window.

faq.question: Is this skill only for authorized testing?
faq.answer: Yes. The workflows should only be used on systems where you have explicit written permission.

faq.question: Does the skill run scanners automatically?
faq.answer: No. It is a guide with examples and methodology, not an automation script.

faq.question: Why is the audit risk high?
faq.answer: The guide includes active scanning, wireless attack, cracking, privileged command, and Metasploit workflows.

faq.question: Can it help with compliance audits?
faq.answer: Yes. It covers tools and report concepts that can support audit evidence collection.

faq.question: Does it replace a professional penetration tester?
faq.answer: No. It provides reference workflows, but expert judgment is required for scope, validation, and reporting.

faq.question: How should sensitive scan data be handled?
faq.answer: Keep results in approved storage, redact secrets, and share reports only with authorized stakeholders.
